instructions: The coach hits fungoes that force the fielder to run through the ball. The player must field it one-handed and throw across their body or on the run. This advances the "On the Run" drill by adding the unpredictability of a batted ball.

goal: To develop soft hands and a stable base by teaching the infielder to 'stick' the backhand catch without stabbing at the ball.
description: Begin in a preset backhand fielding position, with your glove low and open to the ground. As the ball is rolled in, receive it cleanly in the pocket of your glove and 'stick' the catch, freezing your position upon receiving the ball. This action emphasizes keeping the glove hand steady and absorbing the ball's force without stabbing at it, ensuring a secure catch.
instructions: The fielder starts in a static backhand stance with their glove on the ground. A partner rolls a ball, and the fielder simulates the "pick" and holds the position. This isolates the glove mechanics and body angle required to field a backhand cleanly.

goal: To develop clean glove action and muscle memory for securing ground balls on the backhand side.
description: Start in a pre-set backhand fielding stance, with your glove already positioned open and low to the ground on your backhand side. As the ball is rolled, focus on attacking it with your glove, using a firm 'raking' motion from outside-in to secure the ball into the pocket. This drill isolates the final action of the backhand play, allowing for repetition and focus on proper glove mechanics without the need for complex footwork.
instructions: The fielder starts in a backhand position and practices the "raking" motion (pulling the glove through the ball) as a partner rolls it. This helps develop the technique for fielding balls on the run where the glove cannot stop.

goal: To improve an infielder's hand speed, reaction time, and ability to receive the ball with soft, absorbent hands.
description: From a low, athletic fielding position, a player prepares to receive a series of rapid tosses from a coach or partner standing a few feet away. The player focuses on using quick, soft hands to catch each ball cleanly out in front of their body, funnelling it to their center. This drill emphasizes minimal body movement, forcing the player to rely on efficient hand-eye coordination and swift glove-to-hand transfer.
instructions: Fielders stand close to a wall or a partner who throws rapid-fire feeds. The fielder must catch and transfer the ball instantly. The goal is to reduce the time the ball spends in the glove, essential for turning double plays.

goal: To improve an infielder's reaction time, footwork, and ability to handle multiple plays in quick succession under pressure.
description: A coach hits a rapid succession of ground balls to an infielder, who must field each one cleanly and make a quick throw to a designated base. The player should maintain a ready athletic stance, focusing on efficient footwork to move to each ball and a quick, clean exchange to get the ball out of the glove. This drill simulates the fast pace of a real game, forcing the infielder to react and reset quickly between plays.
instructions: The coach hits ground balls to a line of fielders one after another with no pause. As soon as one player fields the ball, the next ball is hit. This high-intensity drill builds conditioning, reaction time, and forces players to clear the area quickly.

goal: To develop proper footwork for fielding ground balls and to create momentum and direction towards the target.
description: An infielder starts in a ready position. A coach rolls a ground ball to their right, forcing them to move laterally. The player shuffles to the ball by stepping with their right foot, then their left, keeping their shoulders square to the ball. After fielding the ball, they continue the right-left shuffle to create momentum towards their target before making a throw.
instructions: A footwork drill where the fielder approaches a ground ball with a "right-left-field" rhythm. They practice stepping right, then left, then fielding the ball in the center of their stance. This establishes the proper timing and momentum for fielding routine grounders.

goal: To develop proper footwork and body control for moving laterally to get into an optimal fielding position for ground balls.
description: An infielder begins in a ready stance. A coach hits a medium-paced groundball to either side of the player, forcing them to move laterally. The player executes a quick 'right-left' shuffle with their feet to get their body centered and in front of the ball before fielding it cleanly out in front. After securing the ball, the player should practice a smooth transition into a throwing position.
instructions: The coach hits fungoes, and the fielder must time their approach to field the ball using the "right-left" footwork pattern. This connects the footwork drill to live batted balls, teaching players to adjust their speed to arrive on time.

goal: To develop an infielder's quick-twitch reactions, footwork, and glove skills when fielding hard-hit balls at close range.
description: A coach with a fungo bat stands a short distance in front of a semi-circle of infielders in their ready positions. The coach hits hard, rapid-fire ground balls and short hops to each player in succession, challenging their reaction time and forcing them to move their feet to get in front of the ball. This drill emphasizes fielding the ball cleanly out in front of the body and executing a quick, efficient transfer from glove to throwing hand.
instructions: Fielders form a semi-circle around the coach, who hits short, controlled fungoes to each player in rapid succession. The focus is on quick hands and feet in a confined area. It allows for high repetition and close coach feedback.

goal: To develop the ability to make a strong and accurate throw after fielding a backhanded ground ball, requiring a quick spin and body control.
description: The shortstop fields a ground ball on their backhand side, planting their glove-side foot and spinning their body toward the target. As they spin, they bring the ball to their throwing hand and make a strong, accurate throw to first base. This drill emphasizes a quick, fluid transition from fielding to throwing on a challenging play.
instructions: The shortstop fields a ball in the hole (deep backhand), plants the right foot, and spins toward the outfield to throw to first. This move is necessary when momentum carries the player away from the target. The drill focuses on balance and a strong throw after the spin.

goal: To improve a shortstop's ability to create a direct throwing lane and make powerful throws on balls hit to their glove side.
description: From the shortstop position, field a ground ball hit to your glove side. Execute a 'drop step' by planting your glove-side foot and dropping your throwing-side foot back and behind to open up your hips towards the target. This creates momentum and alignment, allowing you to make a strong and accurate throw to the base without shuffling your feet.
instructions: The shortstop executes a drop step to field a deep ground ball or pop-up. After catching, they plant and throw to the target. This improves range and the ability to convert defensive plays on balls hit over the fielder's head.

goal: To develop quick, efficient transfers and accurate underhand feeds for middle infielders on plays around the second base bag.
description: The shortstop fields a ground ball moving towards the second base bag, maintaining momentum through the play. They execute a firm underhand flip from the glove to the chest of the second baseman covering the bag. This drill emphasizes a quick, clean exchange and a precise toss that leads the receiver into the base for a force out or to initiate a double play.
instructions: The shortstop fields a ground ball near the bag and practices the underhand flip to

goal: To develop the footwork, body control, and quick hands necessary to make an accurate flip to second base for a potential double play.
description: From the shortstop position, attack a ground ball rolled to your forehand side, fielding it with two hands out in front. As you approach the ball, focus on creating momentum towards second base. Without standing up, execute a quick and controlled underhand flip from your glove, aiming for the second baseman's chest to facilitate a smooth turn for a double play.

goal: To develop an infielder's ability to move laterally, field a ground ball cleanly, and execute a quick, accurate throw.
description: An infielder starts in a ready position as a coach hits a ground ball with a fungo bat just to their side. The player shuffles laterally to get their body in front of the ball, gathers it cleanly into their glove, and then makes a strong, accurate throw to a designated base. This drill emphasizes proper footwork and the smooth transition from fielding to throwing.

goal: To develop the proper footwork and aggressive mentality needed to attack slowly hit ground balls.
description: From their ready position, the infielder charges a slowly rolled ball from a coach or partner. The player should focus on timing their approach to field the ball on a good hop, keeping their feet moving through the play. After securing the ball, they continue their momentum towards first base to make a strong, accurate throw.

goal: To develop the technique and confidence needed to properly approach and field slowly hit ground balls.
description: A coach hits slow rolling ground balls with a fungo bat to infielders at their respective positions. The fielder must aggressively charge the ball, field it out in front of their body, and make a strong, accurate throw to first base. This drill emphasizes using proper footwork to create momentum towards the target and ensures a quick, clean transfer from the glove to the throwing hand.

goal: To improve an infielder's ability to read game situations, communicate effectively with teammates, and execute the correct defensive play as a cohesive unit.
description: With infielders at every position, coaches will hit a variety of ground balls to initiate live game scenarios. Players must read the play, communicate with teammates, and execute the proper defensive assignments, such as turning a double play or making a force out. This drill emphasizes quick decision-making and fluid teamwork across the entire infield.

goal: Teach hitters to load into the back hip with more control before launching forward.
description: Set up two tees — one in the middle of the plate (slightly off the front leg) and one behind the hitter near the back knee, angled with a baseball underneath. A coach or partner observes balance, direction, and load mechanics
instructions: Set up two tees — one in the middle of the plate (slightly off the front leg) and one behind the hitter near the back knee, angled with a baseball underneath. A coach or partner observes balance, direction, and load mechanics

goal: Build better shoulder control and loading pattern by progressing from exaggerated positions into a normal stance.
instructions: The hitter progresses through three stages using a tee set in front of the middle of the plate, beginning at a 90-degree open stance where the focus is on keeping the front shoulder closed and resisting flying open. At both the 90- and 45-degree stages, the hitter uses a shoulder-width stance with no stride to isolate the load into the back hip. The second stage moves to a 45-degree angle, and the final stage progresses into a standard batting stance, carrying over the same loading and shoulder control. This drill is ideal for hitters who struggle to load properly into the back hip or tend to fly open with the front shoulder.

goal: To develop body control and a consistent, powerful posture at the top of the pitching delivery.
description: Start on the pitching rubber with a slight weight bias on your drive leg. Lift your lead leg to your balance point, pausing for a full three to five seconds to ensure your posture is stable and your head is centered over your back leg. If you lose balance, reset and repeat the hold, focusing on a strong core and a controlled, deliberate motion before proceeding with your throw.
instructions: One player is on the mound (mound optional, flat ground can be used) and one player is catching (or a screen can be used). The player starts on the pitching rubber with 60% of their weight on the drive leg and 40% on the lift leg. Once the weight is distributed properly, the player lifts the leg to get into the balance position and holds the stance for 3 to 5 seconds; if the player is unable to stay balanced, have them start over. The player begins the drill with 5 dry repetitions (without throwing) to make sure mechanics are correct. After the dry repetitions, the player goes through the drill again, this time throwing the ball to the catcher by driving down the mound from the balance position. Players should have at least 5 dry repetitions and then at least 10 throwing repetitions.

goal: To develop a consistent and efficient arm action that promotes throwing accuracy and reduces the risk of injury.
description: Begin in a balanced pitching stance with the ball in your glove at your chest. Break your hands and simulate your throwing motion in slow motion, focusing on a consistent and efficient arm path. Concentrate on achieving a proper 'scap load' position before moving through a fluid, circular motion to a high release point, building muscle memory for a smooth delivery.

goal: To enhance the pitcher's natural arm speed and develop a more efficient, quick arm path to the release point.
description: From a standing or kneeling position, the pitcher performs rapid, controlled arm circles both forward and backward, focusing on a loose and fluid motion. The emphasis is on moving the arm as quickly as possible without tension, using a towel or a light training ball to ensure the focus remains on speed rather than force. This drill isolates the arm action to build neuromuscular speed and endurance.

goal: To develop a stable and repeatable balance point, which is crucial for consistent mechanics and command.
description: From the stretch position, the pitcher will come set, lift their lead leg to a balanced position, and hold for a count of three to five seconds before returning to the set position. The focus is on maintaining a stable head and a controlled posture over the landing leg without wobbling. This drill should be repeated multiple times to build muscle memory and core stability.

goal: To isolate the upper body, developing proper core engagement and extension in the pitching motion without lower body involvement.
description: Begin in a balanced, athletic stance with your glove-side shoulder aimed at your target, mimicking your landing position. Your throwing-side foot is anchored behind you as if on the pitching rubber. Execute the throw by focusing on upper body mechanics, emphasizing core engagement and full extension through the release, all while keeping your back foot firmly planted to maintain balance.

goal: To refine upper body mechanics and improve balance by isolating the throwing motion from the lower half.
description: Begin in a balanced, athletic stance with your feet shoulder-width apart, simulating the 'set' position. Without moving your lower body, isolate your upper half and execute your full pitching motion, focusing on arm path and shoulder rotation. Concentrate on maintaining a stable core and a consistent release point throughout the movement.

goal: To train outfielders to confidently track and catch fly balls while effectively shielding their eyes from direct sunlight.
description: The drill starts with an outfielder positioned in their ready stance as a coach throws a high fly ball directly into the sun's path. The player must track the ball while using their glove to shield their eyes from the glare, timing the catch perfectly without losing sight of the ball. This exercise simulates the challenge of making a play when the sun is a major visual obstruction, forcing the player to rely on technique and feel.

goal: To develop the fundamental footwork and body control required for an outfielder to properly field a ground ball and transition into a throw.
description: An outfielder approaches a ground ball, chopping their feet to get into a controlled 'break down' position with their glove-side foot forward. As they receive the ball, they 'come through' it, securing the catch out in front and transitioning their feet and body into a powerful and aligned throwing position. This drill emphasizes fielding the ball cleanly and preparing for a quick, accurate throw.

goal: To develop a smooth and repeatable hand break, ensuring a consistent arm path and proper timing in the pitching delivery.
description: Begin in a standard pitching stance, coming to a balanced position. As you initiate your motion, concentrate on a clean and efficient separation of your hands. The throwing hand should move down, back, and up in a fluid arc, while the glove hand provides balance and direction toward the target. This exercise isolates the critical 'hand break' to build muscle memory for a consistent arm action.

goal: To develop lower-body strength and explosive power from the ground up, improving drive and balance in the pitching delivery.
description: Sit on the edge of a sturdy chair or stool with your feet in your pitching stance, simulating your position on the mound. Drive up from the chair as you begin your throwing motion, focusing on using your legs and glutes to generate power. Explode out of the seated position, ensuring your hips and shoulders rotate in sequence toward the target. This drill isolates the lower half, forcing the pitcher to engage their leg muscles for momentum rather than relying solely on their arm.

goal: To develop a repeatable change-up grip that maximizes deception and control.
description: This drill focuses on finding a comfortable and effective change-up grip. Experiment with various grips like the circle change, three-finger, or vulcan change, focusing on how each feels in your hand and upon release. The key is to maintain your natural arm speed and slot, allowing the grip itself to reduce the pitch's velocity and create deception.

goal: To maximize throwing velocity and accuracy from the outfield by creating linear momentum toward the target.
description: After fielding the ball, an outfielder takes a small, athletic hop forward, landing on their throwing-side foot while bringing their glove-side foot forward. This explosive step helps to align the body toward the target and generate momentum through the lower half. The player then transfers this energy into a powerful and accurate throw, ensuring the ball reaches its destination with velocity.

goal: To prepare the arm for pitching by doing a band workout to stretch and strengthen.
description: Secure an exercise band to a stable object at hip height, then perform a series of internal and external rotations with your throwing arm to stretch and strengthen the shoulder. The routine includes a variety of movements at different heights to ensure a comprehensive warm-up. This drill is designed to prepare the arm for the demands of pitching through a dynamic warm-up.

goal: To develop and reinforce proper pitching mechanics, arm care, and consistency in a low-stress environment.
description: From a flat ground position, the pitcher will throw to a catcher at a reduced distance, focusing on clean and repeatable mechanics. The pitcher should concentrate on maintaining a stable head position, a smooth and controlled arm action, and a balanced follow-through. This drill emphasizes precision and consistency over velocity, allowing the pitcher to build a solid mechanical foundation without the strain of throwing from a mound.

goal: To build a consistent, repeatable throwing motion and establish a solid mechanical foundation before progressing to more intensive pitching work.
description: Begin close to your partner, focusing on a clean and efficient arm path with a full follow-through. Use a four-seam grip and concentrate on spinning the ball with backspin. As you feel loose, gradually increase the distance, ensuring your mechanics remain consistent and your lower half is engaged to support the throw.

goal: To disguise the bunt until the last moment and beat out the throw by placing the ball in a difficult spot for the defense to field.
description: As the pitcher starts their delivery, the batter takes a crossover step with their back foot, simultaneously opening their hips and shoulders toward the field. The bat is brought to a level position, with the top hand guiding the barrel to angle the ball down the desired baseline. The objective is to make contact with the ball out in front of the plate while already in motion, allowing the batter to get a quick start out of the box.

goal: To develop a quick and efficient first step, enabling the outfielder to react and get in position to catch deep fly balls. (Edited inline)
description: Set up three cones in a triangle, with the player starting at the apex. On the coach's signal, the player executes a drop step, opening their hips and shoulders toward one of the back cones, then sprints to that cone. This drill emphasizes creating a direct line to the ball and gaining ground efficiently on balls hit over the outfielder's head.

goal: Goal: Focus on having a good extension and a smooth follow-through.
description: From a set position on a flat surface, the pitcher simulates a full throwing motion, focusing on a clean and repeatable arm action without the strain of a mound. The pitcher should concentrate on maintaining balance, achieving good extension toward the target, and finishing with a smooth, controlled follow-through. This drill emphasizes consistent mechanics over velocity, making it ideal for warm-ups and reinforcing proper form.

goal: To develop the fundamental skills of tracking, judging, and catching fly balls in the outfield.
description: From a ready athletic position, the outfielder reads the trajectory of a fly ball hit by a coach or partner. The player should take a direct route to the ball, getting their body in position to make the catch. Emphasis is placed on using proper footwork to get under the ball and securing it with two hands.

goal: To develop a fundamentally sound and aggressive approach to fielding ground balls in the outfield.
description: From a ready position, the outfielder charges a ground ball hit directly at them. The player should focus on approaching the ball under control, breaking down with their glove-side foot forward, and keeping their eyes on the ball as they field it out in front of their body. After securing the ball in their glove, they should cover it with their throwing hand to prepare for a quick transition.

goal: To develop a consistent and efficient hand separation, synchronizing the arm action with the lower body's movement to improve pitching mechanics and velocity.
description: From the stretch position, the pitcher comes to a set position and then lifts their leg. As the lead leg begins its descent, the hands separate from the glove in a downward and outward motion, keeping the fingers on top of the ball. This drill emphasizes a smooth and properly timed hand break, ensuring the throwing arm is in the correct position as the body moves toward the plate.

goal: Work on balance and weight transfer in the direction of the target
description: Begin in your pitching stance with a slightly heavier ball, such as a weighted baseball or softball, in your glove hand to create a 'heavy' front side sensation. As you move through your delivery, focus on keeping your front shoulder and glove arm directed towards the target for as long as possible before rotation. The added weight provides a physical cue to maintain a strong, stable front side, preventing your upper body from flying open too early.
instructions:
Description of the Drill:

Player sets up at least 10 feet from a target of their choosing (spot on the fence, sign, etc.)
Player should do repetitions in both the windup and stretch positions
Player goes through a dry delivery (without a ball) and pauses when the lift leg hits the ground
Player should make sure the chin and chest are positioned over the front knee with glove side in a strong position
Most of the body weight should be moving forward toward the target
Each player should complete 10 repetitions in the windup and stretch positions
This drill should be done daily as a check point for pitchers

goal: To develop proper hip sequencing and direction into foot-strike, promoting a powerful and efficient throwing motion.
description: From the peak of your leg lift, focus on setting the angle of your hips by driving your back glute toward the target. This initial move should be the catalyst for your entire throwing motion, ensuring your lower half leads the way. By properly sequencing this movement, you will create a more powerful and efficient energy transfer from the ground up, leading to increased velocity and better command.

goal: To enhance a pitcher's body awareness and ability to make targeted mechanical adjustments.
description: This drill emphasizes deliberate practice over velocity. The pitcher focuses on a single mechanical point for each throw, such as hand position or follow-through, while throwing at a reduced intensity. Each repetition is a conscious effort to execute that specific movement perfectly, building a stronger mind-body connection.

goal: To safely build arm strength, improve endurance, and reinforce sound throwing mechanics through a structured progression.
description: Begin by making 15-20 easy throws from a distance of 45-60 feet, focusing on a clean arm action and consistent release point. After a short rest, increase the distance by 15-20 feet and repeat the set, ensuring you maintain the same smooth mechanics without overthrowing. This progression helps pitchers build arm strength and endurance gradually while reinforcing proper throwing fundamentals.

goal: To develop a dynamic, full-body pitching delivery that improves momentum and maximizes arm speed through proper kinetic sequencing.
description: From the windup, the pitcher should focus on creating a continuous and explosive motion toward the plate, avoiding any pauses. The drill emphasizes a strong drive from the back leg and leading the movement with the front hip to generate momentum. The throwing arm should remain relaxed and follow the lower body's rotation, ensuring it comes through late to create a powerful, whip-like action.

goal: To build foundational arm strength, improve throwing mechanics, and promote a loose, fluid arm action over extended distances.
description: Begin by playing catch at a comfortable distance, focusing on a loose and relaxed arm motion. Gradually extend the distance between you and your partner, throwing the ball with a high arc to ensure you are not throwing on a line. As you reach your maximum comfortable distance, work on maintaining a fluid, full range of motion on each throw before gradually decreasing the distance to cool down.

goal: To develop a consistent arm action, proper upper-body rotation, and a repeatable release point without interference from the lower half.
description: Begin in a balanced position with your throwing-side knee on the ground and your glove-side foot planted firmly in front. Without using your lower body, focus on a clean, fluid arm path, rotating your torso and shoulders to deliver the ball towards the target. This drill isolates the upper half, forcing you to concentrate on proper arm mechanics and a consistent release point out in front of your body.

goal: To develop the fundamental techniques for aggressively charging, properly fielding, and quickly transitioning into a throw for ground balls hit to the outfield.
description: From a ready position, the outfielder charges a ground ball hit by a coach or partner, focusing on taking an aggressive but controlled angle. The player should break down with feet apart to get their body in front of the ball and field it cleanly out in front. After securing the ball, the player executes a proper crow-hop to simulate a strong and accurate throw back to the infield.

goal: To develop a strong and repeatable body position that maximizes leverage and explosive force in the pitching delivery.
description: From a balanced starting position, the pitcher lifts their lead leg and drives toward the plate, freezing at the moment their front foot lands. In this power position, the throwing arm should be up and back in a loaded 'L' shape, with hips open and eyes on the target. Hold this pose for a few seconds to build muscle memory before following through without a ball.

goal: To develop the skill of bunting for a base hit by using a firm, controlled push to place the ball offensively.
description: From your batting stance, pivot to square your shoulders to the pitcher as the ball is delivered, bringing the bat out front. Instead of absorbing the pitch for a soft sacrifice, firmly guide the bat forward through the ball upon impact. The objective is to push the ball with pace past the pitcher and charging infielders, aiming for an open area on the infield for a base hit.

goal: To improve an outfielder's ability to read, react, and adjust to fly balls hit in multiple directions.
description: The outfielder starts in a ready position while the coach stands in front of them with two balls. The coach throws one ball over the outfielder's left shoulder and the other over their right, forcing them to drop-step and adjust their route to catch the ball. This drill teaches outfielders to read the ball's trajectory and adjust their path accordingly, emphasizing efficient footwork and glove presentation.

goal: To train outfielders to make powerful and precise throws from various angles while on the run.
description: An outfielder starts by backpedaling away from the infield, then breaks at a 45-degree angle to simulate fielding a ball on the run. As they catch the ball, they will open their hips and make a strong, accurate throw to a designated base, mimicking the motion of a quarterback throwing on the move. This drill emphasizes a quick transition from fielding to throwing while maintaining momentum and accuracy.

goal: To develop an outfielder's ability to properly execute a drop step, efficiently track a deep fly ball, and make a difficult over-the-shoulder catch.
description: The outfielder starts in a ready position while a coach or partner stands behind them, acting as the 'quarterback.' The quarterback throws a ball high and deep over the outfielder's head, forcing them to execute a drop step, turn their hips, and run to track the ball. The player must locate the ball in the air and make a clean over-the-shoulder catch, simulating a game situation where a fly ball is hit over their head.

goal: To teach outfielders the proper footwork and running path for efficiently tracking and catching deep fly balls hit over their head.
description: An outfielder starts in a ready position as a coach throws a deep fly ball over their head, forcing them to retreat. The player will take a drop step with the foot on the side the ball is thrown and run a curved "rainbow" route to get behind the ball. The focus is on maintaining eye contact with the ball while running the arc, allowing the player to catch the ball while moving forward toward their target.

goal: To develop an outfielder's ability to get a quick and accurate jump on the ball by improving their reaction time and first-step efficiency.
description: From a ready athletic position, the outfielder's focus is entirely on the coach or hitter. As the ball is hit or thrown, the player reads the angle and speed off the bat to take a quick, explosive first step in the correct direction—forward, back, left, or right. The drill is completed after the initial movement, emphasizing the 'read and react' phase over catching the ball.

goal: To develop an outfielder's ability to aggressively attack and field a short hop while on the move, turning a difficult play into a routine out.
description: An outfielder charges forward on a ball thrown or hit on a short hop just in front of them. The player must maintain momentum and body control, getting their glove down at the last second to secure the ball on the run. This drill forces the player to read the bounce quickly and field the ball cleanly in stride, simulating a do-or-die play on a sinking liner.

goal: To master the technique of advancing a baserunner by strategically placing a bunt in fair territory, sacrificing the batter for the benefit of the team.
description: Begin by squaring your shoulders to the pitcher as they deliver the ball, presenting the bat early to provide a clear target. Angle the bat to direct the ball towards the desired side of the infield, either first or third base. The objective is to absorb the pitch's energy by 'catching' the ball with the bat, causing it to be deadened upon impact and roll a short distance away from the fielders.

goal: To teach the batter how to execute a successful bunt that puts pressure on the defense and allows a runner to score from third base.
description: With a runner on third base, the batter sets the angle of the bat early, keeping it at the top of the strike zone to bunt the top of the baseball. The focus is on bunting the ball to a specific location, forcing the defense to make a play and allowing the runner from third to score. This drill emphasizes precision and execution over power or surprise.

goal: To develop a hitter's ability to execute a successful sacrifice bunt in a high-pressure, game-like scenario.
description: Set up with runners on first and second base to simulate a live game situation where advancing the runners is critical. As the pitch is delivered, the batter must execute a firm, well-placed bunt down either the first or third baseline, focusing on creating a difficult play for the charging infielders. This drill emphasizes precise bat control and the ability to read the defense while executing under pressure.

goal: To develop the confidence and technique required to aggressively charge and securely field short-hopped ground balls in the outfield.
description: An outfielder charges a ground ball thrown by a coach or partner, focusing on an aggressive approach to field the ball on a short hop. The player should concentrate on keeping their eyes on the ball, maintaining a low body position, and using soft hands to receive the ball cleanly just after the bounce.

goal: To develop a consistent hand position that promotes true four-seam backspin and a downward plane on the fastball.
description: From a balanced throwing stance, the pitcher extends their arm toward the target, positioning the hand so the knuckles face upward and are visible to the catcher. While maintaining this hand position, the pitcher executes their arm circle, focusing on keeping the hand on top of the baseball through the entire motion. This drill simplifies the throwing action to emphasize proper wrist and hand alignment, ensuring the pitcher stays behind the ball to generate clean backspin upon release.

goal: To enhance an outfielder's agility, acceleration, and ability to take efficient routes to the ball in all directions.
description: Set up four cones in a ten-by-ten yard square and start in the center. On the coach's signal, sprint to touch a designated cone, then immediately return to the center before sprinting to the next cone in the sequence. This drill emphasizes explosive first steps and maintaining body control while changing direction at high speed.

goal: To improve throwing accuracy and develop a repeatable, efficient arm action by removing lower body variables.
description: Begin by standing with your feet shoulder-width apart, directly facing your target with your body completely squared up. Without any stride or lower body rotation, isolate your arm and execute a clean, fluid throw, focusing on the path of the ball and your follow-through. This exercise strips down the pitching motion to its essential upper-body mechanics, forcing a focus on a consistent release point and arm slot.

goal: To develop a consistent and balanced stride, ensuring proper body alignment and direction towards home plate.
description: From the stretch position, the pitcher lifts their lead leg and strides forward, focusing on landing their foot on a specific, marked spot on the ground. The emphasis is on maintaining balance and direction toward the target throughout the movement, without actually throwing a ball. This allows the pitcher to isolate and repeat the stride portion of their delivery, building muscle memory for a consistent landing point.

goal: To develop teamwork, communication, and efficiency between outfielders on long throws from the gaps, ensuring the ball gets to the infield quickly and accurately.
description: Two outfielders line up in a tandem position, one directly behind the other, spaced about 15-20 feet apart. On a ball hit into the gap, the lead outfielder pursues and fields the ball while the trailing outfielder moves into a direct line between the lead outfielder and the intended base. The lead outfielder makes a strong, accurate throw to the trailing outfielder who then acts as a relay to make the final throw to the base.

goal: To develop a versatile bunting skillset by providing high-volume, situational repetitions of various bunt types.
description: Set up multiple stations around the infield, each focused on a specific bunting objective like sacrifice bunts, bunts for a hit, or drag bunts. Players rotate through each station, receiving pitches from a coach or machine and executing the designated bunt. This drill allows for high-repetition practice of various bunting techniques in a controlled setting.

goal: To develop upper body strength, proper arm mechanics, and throwing accuracy for pitchers.
description: From a kneeling or seated position to eliminate lower-body involvement, the player focuses solely on the upper body throwing motion. Emphasis should be placed on a smooth, powerful arm action, complete shoulder rotation, and a full follow-through after releasing the ball. This drill isolates the upper half to build pure arm strength and reinforce correct pitching mechanics.

goal: To help pitchers develop a quicker, more efficient delivery from the stretch while maintaining control and composure.
description: Begin in the set position with your glove-side foot on the rubber and hands together at the belt. Come to a complete, balanced stop for a full second before separating your hands and delivering a pitch to the plate. Focus on maintaining a consistent rhythm and a quick, compact motion to minimize the runner's lead and prevent stolen bases.

goal: Develop a hitter's timing and adjustability to different pitch speeds without changing their core swing mechanics.
description: Set up three home plates in a line toward the pitcher, spaced approximately 3–5 feet apart (typically at 10, 15, and 20 feet from the tosser).
A coach stands behind an L-screen and delivers front-toss pitches at a constant speed while the batter takes 3–5 swings at each plate, starting from the back and progressing forward (or vice versa).

Because the toss speed never changes, the distance determines the difficulty: the back plate simulates a slow off-speed pitch requiring the hitter to stay back and wait, while the front plate simulates a high-velocity fastball that forces the hitter to load earlier and use quicker hands.
The goal is for the hitter to maintain their core swing mechanics while adjusting their timing and rhythm so the front foot is down before contact at every station.
instructions: Set up three home plates in a line toward the pitcher, spaced approximately 3–5 feet apart (typically at 10, 15, and 20 feet from the tosser). A coach stands behind an L-screen and delivers front-toss pitches at a constant speed while the batter takes 3–5 swings at each plate, starting from the back and progressing forward (or vice versa). Because the toss speed never changes, the distance determines the difficulty: the back plate simulates a slow off-speed pitch requiring the hitter to stay back and wait, while the front plate simulates a high-velocity fastball that forces the hitter to load earlier and use quicker hands. The goal is for the hitter to maintain their core swing mechanics while adjusting their timing and rhythm so the front foot is down before contact at every station.

goal: To teach younger hitters how to properly stride, transfer weight, and create upper- and lower-body separation so they can consistently get into a strong, balanced hitting position.
description: The hitter starts with their feet together and the knob of the bat at their belly button.
Stride forward while creating separation with the upper body and freeze in a strong loaded position.
Coach checks balance, posture, and alignment before resetting.
Repeat this sequence until the hitter understands the movement.
Progress to swinging: start the same way—feet together, knob at the belly button—feel the stride, weight transfer, and separation, then swing away.
Walking variation: stride and separate, freeze, bring the back foot up to the front foot, and continue walking forward while maintaining balance and separation.
instructions: The hitter starts with their feet together and the knob of the bat at their belly button, then strides forward while creating separation with the upper body and freezes in a strong loaded position as the coach checks balance, posture, and alignment before resetting; this sequence is repeated until the hitter understands the movement, after which they progress to swinging by starting the same way—feet together, knob at the belly button, feeling the stride, weight transfer, and separation—then swinging away, and a walking variation can be added where the hitter strides and separates, freezes, brings the back foot up to the front foot, and continues walking forward while maintaining balance and separation.

goal: The goal of the slow-to-fast drill is to teach hitters how to control their body and sequencing early in the swing, then gradually add speed without losing balance, posture, or connection.
instructions: Start the hitter in a normal stance and have them begin the swing in slow motion, focusing on a controlled load, balanced stride, and proper separation as the lower half begins to move before the hands, then once the front foot is about to land, have them accelerate the swing to full speed through contact and finish, emphasizing staying connected and balanced throughout; the coach should cue the hitter to feel smooth and controlled early and fast and explosive late, reinforcing that the swing builds speed progressively rather than being rushed from the start.

goal: The goal of one-handed hitting drills is to isolate and strengthen the roles of the top and bottom hands in the swing so hitters improve barrel control, bat path, and power while developing better feel, coordination, and efficient sequencing that transfers into a stronger, more consistent two-handed swing.
instructions: Using a lighter training bat, have the hitter take swings with only one hand on the bat at a time—top hand to focus on guiding and controlling the barrel through the zone, and bottom hand to focus on driving the swing with proper rotation and whip—starting from a normal stance with a controlled load, swinging smoothly through contact with balance and control, performing the drill off a tee or soft toss for 2–3 sets of 10–15 quality swings per hand, then finishing with a few two-handed swings to blend the feels back into a full swing.

goal: The goal of the crossover hitting drill is to improve lower-body control, sequencing, and balance by forcing the hitter to feel proper weight transfer and hip rotation, helping eliminate spinning, lunging, or drifting while reinforcing that the lower half leads the swing and creates a stable base for the hands to work efficiently.
instructions: Have the hitter start with their feet crossed (back foot crossed over in front of the front foot) in a narrow stance, then as the pitch is delivered or before the swing off a tee, the hitter steps back into a normal stance, lands under control, and swings, focusing on staying balanced, driving from the ground up, and letting the hips initiate the swing before the hands fire, performing controlled reps off a tee or soft toss and emphasizing smooth footwork, clean sequencing, and a stable finish.

goal: The goal of the Elevated Tee Drill is to help hitters adjust to high pitch locations by improving swing plane, barrel accuracy, and posture, teaching them to stay on top of the ball without dropping the barrel or changing their mechanics.
instructions: Place a tee set high near the front of the plate at a height that simulates a pitch at the top of the strike zone, then have the hitter swing from a normal stance focusing on staying balanced, keeping their chest and head level, and matching the barrel to the height of the ball while driving it on a clean line, emphasizing controlled swings and consistent contact without collapsing or over-swinging.

goal: The goal of the step-back drill is to improve rhythm, timing, and lower-half sequencing by teaching hitters how to gather into their back hip and move forward under control, helping eliminate rushing, drifting, and spinning while creating a smooth, powerful swing.
instructions: Have the hitter start in a normal stance, take a small step back with the front foot to create a controlled gather, then move forward into their normal stride and swing, focusing on staying balanced, loading into the back hip, and letting the lower half lead the swing before the hands fire, performing smooth, repeatable reps off a tee or soft toss with emphasis on rhythm and body control.

goal: This drill helps hitters improve their hip rotation control and balance at contact, resulting in a more powerful and effective swing.
instructions: The hitter begins in their normal stance, then strides forward and takes a full swing while allowing the back foot to slide slightly backward behind its starting position, actively resisting the forward momentum of the front hip; at contact, the back foot becomes light and continues to drift back, and immediately after contact the hitter jumps or hops back onto the back foot so the front foot lifts off the ground and the body lands balanced on the heel of the back foot, reinforcing control, balance, and proper weight transfer.

goal: Develop stride control and proper weight distribution by teaching the hitter to stay loaded on the back hip, ride the back leg forward, and create separation without drifting or lunging.
instructions: Start with your feet together at the plate or in front of a tee and hold the bat in your normal stance; lift the front leg into a balanced flamingo position with the back knee slightly bent and the front shoulder closed, then begin striding forward slowly while keeping pressure in the back leg and allowing the body to move forward as the hands stay back; land softly in an athletic position without dumping weight onto the front foot, and once the front foot is down, rotate and swing through the ball under control.

goal: Teach hitters to feel and maintain proper separation by reinforcing scap load, rear-side stability, and the connection between the upper and lower half.
instructions: Set the hitter in their normal stance with the bat held across the chest or in a shortened position, then have them rotate slightly into their load while actively pulling the rear elbow/ scap back as if drawing a bowstring, keeping the front side closed and the chest resisting rotation; hold this stretched position briefly to feel tension between the upper body and lower half, then release into a controlled swing, focusing on keeping the backside from collapsing and maintaining separation until the swing fires.

goal: Hip-separation hitting drill that teaches athletes to fire the hips before the hands
instructions: Start in normal stance, get to  launch position, drive that back knee while keeping your hands back and then fire.

goal: Improve swing sequencing and bat speed by forcing the hitter to use their body instead of swinging with the arms.
instructions: Using a tee or front toss, the hitter takes normal game-like swings with the heavy bat while focusing on turning from the middle, keeping the backside strong, and staying connected through the swing. Emphasize controlled effort, good posture, and balance—no muscling the bat. The goal is clean, sequenced movement, not swing speed. This drill is best for older hitters who struggle with being armsy or out of sequence and is not recommended for younger players.

goal: To help hitters establish consistent pre-pitch rhythm that reduces tension, improves timing, and sets up a dynamic move into the load. The rhythm becomes the tempo engine for the entire swing.
instructions: Begin the drill in a relaxed, athletic stance with loose hands and a calm upper body. Introduce a natural, repeatable rhythm—this could be a light bounce, sway, or hand flow—whatever keeps the hitter loose and engaged. The rhythm should sync with the pitcher’s tempo or imagined timing, helping the hitter transition seamlessly into their load and stride. Emphasize staying fluid, not stiff or mechanical. The key is to build confidence in a pre-pitch routine that prepares the body and mind to fire efficiently. This drill trains hitters to start smooth and finish fast.

goal: Build slow-to-fast rhythm — controlled load/stride (“Kaa”) followed by an explosive launch (“Boom”). Helps hitters stop rushing/drifting and sync foot plant with swing start.
instructions: Set the hitter up on a tee or with easy front toss in their normal stance. On every rep, they say “Kaa…” as they load and stride slowly under control, staying balanced and stacked as they move forward. The instant the front foot lands (foot/heel down), they immediately fire the swing and say “BOOM!” with a fast, aggressive turn—no extra pause. Repeat for multiple reps, coaching the hitter that “Kaa” is smooth and adjustable, while “Boom” is always quick and decisive, with the goal of creating consistent timing and eliminating rushed, forward-leaking swings.

goal: Use your bare hand to catch the ball deep in the strike zone instead of swinging at it. This forces them to keep their head on the ball, improve patience, and develop better hand-eye coordination
instructions: The hitter stands in the batter's box in their normal stance while a partner or machine delivers a front toss. The hitter executes their full load and stride as if they were taking a real swing, but instead of swinging the bat, they track the ball all the way to the hitting zone and catch it with their bare hand. It is crucial to let the ball travel deep into the zone rather than reaching for it, ensuring the hitter maintains proper torso tilt and keeps their eyes focused down the "barrel" to prevent pulling their head off the ball.

goal: The goal of the mirror drill is to develop proper hitting mechanics by providing immediate visual feedback, allowing players to self-correct and internalize key movements.
instructions: To run the mirror drill, position yourself standing in front of a full-length mirror where you can clearly see your entire hitting posture and movements from head to toe. Begin by focusing on the “separation” phase, which refers to the proper positioning and movement of the hands, hips, and shoulders before the swing. Check that your hands are set in the correct position relative to your body and that your hips and shoulders are creating the desired tension or cocked position. Next, progress to the stride drill portion by practicing your initial step forward, ensuring your stride is controlled, balanced, and places you in the correct hitting stance. Watch the mirror to confirm that your stride foot lands properly and that your weight shifts appropriately without overcommitting or losing balance. Then, work on the seesaw drill, which involves simulating the weight transfer during the swing by rocking your weight from your back foot to your front foot in a smooth, controlled motion. Use the mirror to verify that your weight moves correctly and that your posture remains stable throughout the movement. Throughout the drill, do not use a bat or ball; instead, rely solely on visual feedback to adjust and refine your mechanics. Perform multiple repetitions of each component—separation, stride, and seesaw movements—until you can consistently replicate the proper form. The key cue is to focus on the feeling of the correct positions as seen in the mirror and to develop muscle memory by repeating these mechanics slowly and deliberately. This drill can be done anywhere with a mirror and requires no additional equipment, making it practical for daily use and serious player development.

goal: To develop a hitter’s understanding and control of the back foot kickback movement, focusing on proper rhythm, timing, and deceleration during the swing.
instructions: Set up the hitter at a comfortable distance from the tee, ensuring they are neither too far back nor too close to avoid awkward stepping or overstriding. The hitter uses a mini bat, holding it only with their top hand to isolate upper body control and movement. Begin by having the hitter lift their front foot and cross it behind the back foot in a smooth, controlled motion. As the front foot crosses behind, the hitter then swings with one hand, aiming for a solid extension without locking out the elbow — a slight bend is maintained to avoid pushing or overextending. The swing should emphasize a hip hinge, where the hitter slightly bends forward at the hips while the back foot slides behind instead of rotating fully. The movement is slow and deliberate, prioritizing smooth rhythm and timing rather than speed. The hitter should avoid rushing through the kickback; the foot slide behind must be clean and controlled, creating a natural deceleration before the swing’s contact point. Repeat this sequence multiple times, focusing on maintaining a consistent tempo, proper back foot placement, and a balanced finish. The coach should observe for any tendency to rotate the back foot too much or rush the step and provide immediate feedback to correct these errors. The goal is to ingrain the feeling of proper back foot mechanics and rhythm that will translate into improved swing balance and timing during live hitting.

goal: To teach hitters to properly unanchor their back foot during hip rotation, promoting effective weight transfer and preventing getting stuck on the backside.
instructions: This drill exists to solve the common problem where hitters “squish the bug” on their back foot, causing limited hip rotation and poor weight transfer. It helps hitters develop a feeling and visual cue for unanchoring the back foot to improve rotational mechanics.

goal: To develop a proper hip hinge during the load phase and promote controlled forward movement in the hitter’s swing.
instructions: Begin the drill with the hitter in a kneeling position: the front leg is down on the ground, and the back leg is up, positioned comfortably behind. The hitter should hold the bat in their normal hitting stance or grip to simulate the natural setup. From this position, the hitter initiates movement by standing up, focusing on maintaining a hip hinge rather than standing completely upright. The key coaching cues are to keep the load pressure on the inside edge of the back foot, avoiding shifting the knee or hips outside of the back foot’s base. Many hitters tend to either stand straight up too early or allow their knee to drift outside the back foot, which reduces power and control. This drill forces them to engage the back hip properly by hinging at the hips while maintaining pressure inside the back foot, promoting a controlled forward motion into the swing. Repetitions should emphasize quality over quantity, ensuring each stand-up maintains proper hip hinge and foot pressure. The drill can be repeated multiple times per session, focusing on smooth, controlled movements rather than rushing to full standing or swing completion.

goal: To develop proper sequencing in the baseball swing by promoting a strong, stable front leg and effective rotational movement through controlled balance.
instructions: The drill is performed by standing on one leg—specifically, the front leg used during the swing. The athlete lifts the back leg slightly, balancing solely on the front leg to simulate the stance during a swing’s power phase. From this position, the player focuses on closing the front pelvis (hips) first, which mimics the initial forward movement in the swing sequence. Once the hips close toward the pitcher, the athlete then rotates the upper body, including the thoracic spine, to simulate the follow-through of the swing. The drill concludes with the hitter “stopping” in the finish position to assess balance and control. Key cues include maintaining a firm, grounded front leg throughout the movement, feeling the weight “in the ground,” and hinging at the hips to preserve good posture. The player should also work on getting the back shoulder under the chin slightly to enhance rotation and posture. The drill demands a high level of mobility and control, particularly in the hips and thoracic spine, due to the instability created by balancing on one leg. It is designed to train the neuromuscular system to sequence the hips closing before the upper body rotates, a critical component of an efficient, powerful swing. This drill can be repeated multiple times in sets, focusing on quality and control rather than speed. The exact number of reps was not specified in the video, so the coach must tailor this based on the athlete’s mobility and balance capabilities. Being a high-level drill, it should be used with players who have a foundational level of balance and mobility, as it challenges these areas significantly. If a player struggles to maintain posture or balance, they should pause and work on those elements before increasing complexity or speed.

goal: To teach hitters how to effectively integrate hip hinge and shoulder plane mechanics into their swing using the airplane drill.
instructions: Set up with your arms fully extended straight out to the sides, keeping elbows locked. Begin from a slow-motion launch position, focusing on a strong hip hinge and side bend to align your shoulder plane toward the target pitch. Start by positioning the tee at a low height and use your top hand to reach toward the ball, emphasizing the shoulder plane angle that matches the low pitch. Progressively move the tee higher—first to waist level, then to the top of the strike zone—adjusting your side bend and shoulder plane accordingly to match the pitch height. Initially, pause at contact to feel the hinge and plane mechanics, then, as you gain proficiency, swing through completely, maintaining these angles throughout the motion.

goal: To develop a cleaner, more efficient load by simplifying the hitter’s motion and promoting proper hand and bat positioning through the one-handed load drill.
instructions: To perform the one-handed load drill, start in a crossed-over stance with good K posture, ensuring your front shoulder is lower than your back shoulder and your hips are slightly hinged toward your back hip; hold the bat with your back hand in a vertical position so the knob points down toward the catcher, then reach back by moving your hand upward and backward without bringing it forward or losing separation between your hands, maintaining this position as you initiate the swing and continue through a full, fluid motion.

goal: To teach hitters proper hip rotation and separation during their swing for improved power and mechanics
instructions: Start with the hitter standing square to the plate, looking at the pitcher. The hitter's focus should be on turning the core and belly button toward the pitcher without allowing the shoulders or lower body to turn prematurely. This isolates the hips and core, teaching the hitter to create separation between the upper and lower body. Alexa demonstrates this by rotating her hips inward and back outward, maintaining control and preventing the shoulders from flying open too early.

goal: Improve barrel rotation and hand positioning during the swing to create a more efficient and powerful swing path.
instructions:
Start by focusing on turning your belly button toward the pitcher to initiate your swing's rotation. Begin slow and controlled, ensuring your lead elbow (left elbow for right-handed hitters) is up, and the trail elbow is positioned down near your chin. The bat knob should point toward your chin, signaling the correct hand and wrist position. Use your catcher's glove as a reference point to feel where your hands and elbows should be; this helps prevent casting the barrel too early. Start the movement slowly, pulling the bat in with control, then gradually increase speed as you get more comfortable with the mechanics.
Keep repeating this drill until you can consistently maintain the correct elbow angles and bat knob orientation while turning through the swing. The key is smooth, deliberate movement that builds muscle memory before adding full swing speed.

goal: Help hitters develop a proper bat path that produces a more upward swing plane to get the ball in the air, reducing excessive topspin and weak grounders.
instructions: Set up two tees side by side: place the inside tee higher and the outside tee lower. The hitter’s goal is to swing with a bat path that goes from slightly down to up—think of the swing traveling “north to south” rather than flat “east to west.” The key is to swing through the ball without hitting the inside (high) tee on the left side, which forces the bat to stay on an upward plane. This helps prevent a flat swing that creates unwanted topspin or hooks. The hitter should focus on keeping their back elbow away from the body and driving through the ball, which you can clearly see from the outside pitch view. By doing this, players learn to extend their arms properly and direct the bat path upward, promoting better ball launch angles and true backspin. Repeat this drill with controlled swings, making sure to avoid contacting the inside tee while consistently brushing the outside tee at a lower height. This drill is especially helpful for players struggling to get the ball airborne with authority and clean spin.

goal: The goal of the Seesaw to Contact drill is to develop a proper elbow position during the swing and improve the hitter’s ability to reach the point of contact consistently and hold that position.
instructions: To run the Seesaw to Contact drill, start by focusing on “seesawing” your elbows back into the ideal hitting position. This means moving your elbows in a controlled, rocking motion to get them aligned correctly before initiating the swing. Once in position, swing through to the contact point and then freeze your body there. Holding the freeze lets you check your swing mechanics and feel if your elbows are in the right place and if you’ve reached a proper contact position. The drill is about quality over quantity, so take your time with each swing to really lock in the feel of the movement. It’s recommended to do multiple reps, focusing on the smooth seesaw motion of the elbows and holding the contact position long enough to assess your form. This drill is simple but effective for ingraining the muscle memory of good elbow positioning and consistent contact, which are crucial for solid hitting.

goal: This drill helps hitters who struggle with over-rotating their shoulders or hips too early, which often leads to loss of power and poor swing mechanics. It benefits players working on posture, hip hinge, and overall swing control.
instructions:
To do this drill properly

All you need is a bat and your body. Start by standing with your back foot stepping behind you, closing the pelvis slightly. Slowly hinge at the hips and work the bat underneath your shoulders, not around them.

The movement should be slow and controlled, emphasizing that the back shoulder moves under the body, not around it. Repeat the motion slowly to build feel and muscle memory.

goal: Activate the shoulders during the load phase — creating proper rhythm, plane changes, and momentum before the swing.
instructions:
- Get the hitter into their normal stance.
- Bottom hand: Normal grip.
- op hand: Slide it down to hold the bat at the very end.
- Form a strong "L" shape with both arms and elbows.

goal: Improve infield drill performance so that each position can consistently field the ball and make an accurate throw to first base in 4.3 seconds or less per attempt. This will enhance defensive speed, accuracy, and overall team efficiency.
instructions: Fill all infield positions with infielders. Pitchers and catchers can either shag balls in the outfield or assist as catchers for the fungo hitter. Coach 1 should set up beside home plate to hit ground balls, delivering them to each infielder one at a time. Infielders are to field the ball and throw to first base, after which the first baseman tosses the ball to the catcher. Coach 2, or a designated player, will start the timer when the ball is hit and stop it when the first baseman catches the throw. The goal for each position is to get the ball to first base in 4.3 seconds or less. Players should focus on fielding the ball cleanly and playing at game speed. After every rotation, Coach 2 should inform players of their times. Coach 1 continues the drill until each infielder has fielded 10 grounders.

goal: The goal of the drill is to teach the hitter to properly load into the back hip, execute a controlled, balanced stride, and achieve a strong launch position. It also emphasizes creating separation between the lower and upper halves. This is critical because power and consistency come from loading correctly and landing balanced before the swing begins.
instructions:
Place the ball on the tee and set up the net in front of the tee. The hitter starts in a normal stance.

Step 1 — Load: Shift weight into the back hip, allowing the back hip to coil slightly inward while the hands stay back and the head remains centered. The key checkpoint is that the weight stays inside the back leg and does not drift forward; this creates stored energy. 

Step 2 — Stride: The front foot lifts and moves forward under control, landing softly with the front foot slightly open (about 45°), while maintaining balance and avoiding lunging. At landing, the hands are still back, the shoulders remain closed, and the hips are beginning to open. This is the launch position, defined as the point right before hitters explode through the ball. 

Step 3 — Swing and Drive Ball Into Net: After reaching launch position, the hitter rotates, drives the ball into the net, and finishes the full swing. 

Coaching Cues (Critical): Use these exact phrases — “Load into your back hip,” “Control the stride,” “Land balanced,” “Hands stay back,” “Don’t leak forward,” and “Slow load — explosive swing.” 

What This Drill Fixes: This drill most commonly corrects lunging, lack of separation, a weak lower half, hands drifting early, and the absence of a defined launch position.

goal: To develop proper stepping mechanics and scapular activation during the load and stride phase of the baseball swing.
instructions:
Set a PVC pipe vertically into the ground to serve as a reference point. Begin in your normal batting stance, whether open or wide. Focus on initiating the load and stride forward without moving the PVC pipe, ensuring your front side remains stable.

As you stride forward, avoid bringing your hands forward with your body. Instead, step away from the PVC pipe while pulling your hands and your scapula backward to engage your upper rear muscles properly. This helps create separation between the lower and upper body for a stronger swing.

goal: The primary goal of the Band-Assisted Hip Hinge Drill is to force the hitter to maintain a proper hip hinge throughout the swing
instructions: Set the band by having the hitter step inside and place it comfortably around their waist. The coach or partner then holds the other end of the resistance band and crouches directly behind the hitter in the catcher's position. For tee placement, set the tee at a middle- or high-pitch location—avoiding low pitches to prevent the bat from striking the resistance band during the swing. The hitter then enters into the box, takes their stride, and stops in their launch position, ensuring they establish a good stretch and a strong hip hinge with their eyes over their toes. The coach slightly pulls back on the band to create resistance toward the catcher. When executing the swing, the hitter should take a full, aggressive swing at the ball on the tee, maintaining their hip hinge, keeping their eyes over their toes, and staying grounded through the finish.

goal: Tio get hitters to maintain a strong, stable back foot connection to the ground while using a proper hip hinge. This leads to better rotation, more power, and a more controlled swing that finishes in a balanced, extended position.
instructions: Start by having the hitter take a wide, balanced stance with a bat placed horizontally between their feet, roughly at half their height. From a relaxed upper body position, the hitter loads by hinging at the hips and shifting weight into the back hip without letting the back foot roll or lift. Then, they rotate their hips and upper body through the swing, focusing on turning rather than pushing, and finish in an extended, balanced position. The bat acts as a visual and physical cue to keep the feet steady the entire time.

goal: The goal is to train hitters to keep the bat on a flatter, more level path by focusing on hip rotation and staying behind the ball during the swing. main focus: The main focus is on turning the hips properly and maintaining a square setup toward the pitcher while keeping the bat on a flatter plane through contact.
instructions: To do this drill, set up the hitter squared up to the pitcher, emphasizing that they stay behind the ball and focus on turning their hips to flatten the bat path. The hitter should work on maintaining a flat thigh angle and keeping the bat as level as possible during the swing. Maintaining this position throughout the swing helps ingrain the proper plane.

goal: Improve swing mechanics and timing using self toss
instructions:
Toss the ball lightly in front at contact zone.
Swing at 50% speed from launch position to full extension.
Keep feet wide, coiled with hip hinge and proper launch stance.

goal: Increase hip velocity and lower body involvement
instructions: Get into stance, stride, and launch position. Rotate pelvis about 90 degrees towards pitcher in three quick movements. Swing and make contact focusing on hip rotation.

goal: To prevent the hitter from collapsing on their backside, ensuring a proper load that sets the foundation for the swing
instructions: Stand with your back foot against a wall (or a box). Practice coiling by turning your back pocket toward the pitcher. Maintain balance and control; if your knee or hip bumps the wall, you are swaying rather than coiling.
